text:
This paper presents a state-of-the-art approach to sentiment analysis and a new system which can classify short text into positive and negative on message level (e.g. tweets and SMS) as well as on term level (e.g. in relation to a word in a sentence or article). We tackle two different problems: First, on the message level we classify short pieces of text, as they occur in microblogging platforms, SMS and Instant messaging applications. Second, on the term level we decide the sentiment of terms that occur within a text on a longer scale such as for instance online news and discussion forums. Our approach is a supervised learning one, falling into the class of statistical text classification techniques, and it uses a large variety of characteristic attributes for the texts at both message and term level, namely surface form n-grams, semantic clusters as well as a number of derived from their sentiment characteristics.

Our method relies on developing a new generation of high-coverage sentiment lexicons designed for social media. We build these lexicons from millions of tweets that include relevant hashtags and emoticons. We develop separate sentiment lexicons for the negated and non-negated cases to cope with the common challenge of negation, where polarity reversal is not always appropriate.

We report on the Sentiment Analysis in Twitter task (Task 2) at SemEval-2013 where our system achieved the highest performance. Evaluation results are 69.02 and 88.93 for the message-level and term-level scores respectively. The scores improved to 70.45 and 89.50 after post-competition enhancements to the system. In addition to Twitter, we also outperform previous work on an SMS test set and a subset of movie review excerpts. Ablation studies that analyze the contribution of the individual knowledge sources show that the automatically generated domain-specific lexicons yield improvements up to 6.5 percentage points and confirm the importance of domain-specific lexical resources for sentiment analysis in informal text.
